.codex\reprioritization_2026_05_20\review_iter1.txt:15206:.codex\I-cd-009\codex_brief_review_iter5.txt:6214:.codex\I-cd-008\codex_brief_review_iter1.txt:3418:outputs\audit_2026_05_18\all_issues.json:1:[{"body":"## Problem\n\nClaude repeatedly ends a turn with a prose status/next-step report instead of\nthe next tool call, after finishing a unit of work (a PR merge, a grounded\nscan, \"the next step is X\"). The user has flagged this 5+ times\n(2026-05-07 → 2026-05-18: \"why you pause here? any valid reason? figure out a\nway to resolve this constant pausing behaviour\").\n\nEvery prior fix was a *rule* (CLAUDE.md §8.1 banned STATUS blocks; §8.2 banned\nprose-between-merge-and-branch; §8.3.10 banned named rationalizations) — and\neach failed because the pause is a decision made at stop-time and the agent\nrationalizes a fresh exception. Per `failure_28_commits`: \"promises don't\nwork; structural removal does.\" A rule the agent must remember and apply is\nnot structural. The only structural mechanism is one **external to the agent's\nin-the-moment judgment** — a Stop hook.\n\n## Proposed mechanism\n\nA Claude Code `Stop` hook (`.claude/hooks/`, project-scoped per\n`stop_hook_must_be_project_scoped`) that, when the agent attempts to end a\nturn, decides allow-vs-block:\n\n- **Allow the stop** if any of: the last message is a user turn awaiting a\n  reply; a `state/halt_*` marker exists; the transcript shows a Codex iter-5\n  REQUEST_CHANGES; `state/active_issue.json` shows no in-progress work.\n- **Block the stop** (return the `block` decision + a reason) if there is\n  in-progress issue-queue work and none of the allow conditions hold — inject\n  \"continue: the next output is a tool call advancing the issue, not prose.\"\n\n## Hard constraints (this area is delicate)\n\n- **Loop-safety is mandatory.** A Stop hook that always blocks creates an\n  infinite loop. It must bound the number of consecutive blocks and/or only\n  block when the transcript shows a *just-completed* sub-step. See\n  `feedback_no_unbounded_polling_loops`.\n- Per #524 (I-pin-001): the prior `stop_hook_v3.py` canonical-pin check was\n  **deliberately neutralized** for false-firing on Windows CRLF. This hook\n  must not repeat that — no false blocks; when uncertain, allow.\n- Must distinguish \"user just asked a question\" (allow) from \"agent finished a\n  PR and the queue has more\" (block) — the core discriminator.\n- Codex APPROVE on brief + diff; offline test of both the allow and block\n  paths before merge.\n\n## Acceptance\n\n- A `Stop` hook is wired in `.claude/settings.json`, project-scoped, that\n  blocks a turn-end during incomplete issue-queue work with no fired halt.\n- Loop-safety: bounded consecutive blocks; a documented test of the\n  infinite-loop guard.\n- The allow path (user question / halt marker / no active issue) is tested.\n- Does not re-introduce the CRLF false-fire class of bug.\n\nRefs: CLAUDE.md §8.1/§8.2/§8.3.10; `feedback_dont_pause_keep_executing`;\n`failure_28_commits`; #524 (prior stop-hook neutralization).","closedAt":null,"createdAt":"2026-05-18T07:28:59Z","labels":[],"number":589,"state":"OPEN","title":"I-ci-002 — Stop hook to block fake-pause turn-ends during incomplete issue-queue work"},{"body":"## Bug\n\nSurfaced by the #400 (I-beat-001) BEAT-BOTH §-1.1 line-by-line audit — `outputs/audits/I-beat-001/cross_review_v12.md`, claim Q3-C1.\n\nIn the POLARIS Q3 (workforce / gen-AI white-collar employment) report, the gen-AI occupational-exposure decimals **75.5% / 68.4% / 62.6%** are cited to **Goldman Sachs 2023**. The decimals do not match the Goldman Sachs 2023 baseline — they match the **PWBM 2025** (Penn Wharton Budget Model, Eloundou/BLS-derived) exposure table.\n\nDecimals are correct (PWBM published them); the **source attribution is wrong** (citation points at Goldman Sachs 2023).\n\n## Classification\n\nThis is **NOT a fabrication** — the numbers are real and traceable. It is a citation-attribution error caught by the line-by-line audit (Codex independently traced the decimals to PWBM 2025 during cross-review iter14).\n\n## Likely root cause\n\nThe `workforce` scope template / domain backend, or the generator's source-binding for the exposure-statistics claim, attributes the exposure table to the wrong retrieved source. Two issue-ID classes in the current queue do\nnot survive this:\n\n### 1. `-followup` issues collapse onto their parent\n\nIssues `I-rdy-019-followup` (#558), `I-rdy-010-followup` (#537),\n`I-rdy-011-followup` (#539), `I-rdy-016-followup` (#547),\n`I-rdy-017-followup` (#549), `I-gen-004-followup` (#561) — a branch\n`bot/I-rdy-019-followup-*` canonicalizes to `ISSUE_ID = I-rdy-019`, i.e. the\n**parent issue's** (#515's) already-merged `.codex/I-rdy-019/` directory.\nThe gate then reads the parent's `codex_diff.patch` and excludes the wrong\ndir from the canonical-diff computation.\n\n**Observed on #558 / PR #569:** `codex-required` failed —\n`declared (trailer): 17dd8d74…` (read from `.codex/I-rdy-019/codex_diff.patch`,\n#515's file) vs `actual: 63ccfc8f…` (canonical diff that did NOT exclude\n`.codex/I-rdy-019-followup/**`). Worked around by re-cutting the branch +\n`.codex/` dir to a canonical non-colliding id `I-rdy-558` (PR #570, merged).\n\n### 2. `a/b/c` carved issues do not match the regex at all\n\nIssues `I-rdy-014a` / `-014b` / `-014c` (#542/#543/#544): a branch\n`bot/I-rdy-014a` — group 1 would match `I-rdy-014`, leaving a bare `a` with\nno leading `-`, so group 2 `(-[a-z0-9_-]+)?` cannot consume it → the whole\nregex fails to match → the branch falls through to the `elif bot/*` reject\narm and `codex-required` ERRORs the PR outright.\n\n## Acceptance criteria\n\nOne of:\n- (a) The gate's ISSUE_ID derivation supports the `-followup` and `a/b/c`\n  suffix forms as distinct issue IDs (e.g. capture the full\n  `I-<prefix>-<NNN>(-followup|[a-c])` token), with matching `.codex/<id>/`\n  dirs; OR\n- (b) A documented branch-naming convention: every issue — including\n  followups and carved issues — gets a unique canonical `I-<prefix>-<NNN>`\n  branch id (followups pick a fresh number), and the gate docs state it.\n\nThis is a CI-gate change to `codex-required.yml` (a sensitive required\ncheck) — routed to the operator / Codex for the (a)-vs-(b) decision rather\nthan auto-patched in the issue loop.\n\n## Workaround in effect\n\nUntil resolved, the issue loop re-cuts any `-followup` / carved branch to a\ncanonical non-colliding `I-<prefix>-<NNN>` (e.g. `I-rdy-019-followup` →\nbranch `bot/I-rdy-558-test-matrix`, `.codex/I-rdy-558/`).","closedAt":"2026-05-18T23:00:21Z","createdAt":"2026-05-17T20:31:35Z","labels":[],"number":571,"state":"CLOSED","title":"I-ci-001 — codex-required ISSUE_ID regex collapses -followup issue IDs onto the parent (and rejects a/b/c carved IDs)"},{"body":"## Summary\n\nThe `codex_artifact_gate` CI workflow shipped in #535 (I-sec-001, PR #565,\nsquash `f5217344`) is **inert** — it does not run on PRs. A\ndetached fetch-backend task whose Playwright cleanup (`_try_crawl4ai` →\n`_safe_close_crawler` → `__aexit__`) is genuinely wedged would therefore\ndelay — in the theoretical worst case, indefinitely — `asyncio.run`\n**teardown**, *after* all artifacts and the manifest are already written.\n\nIn practice Playwright browser cleanup is slow-worst-case, not\ninfinite-worst-case (CDP has internal timeouts; the browser subprocess is\nSIGTERM'd by Playwright's own process management), so the detached task is\nexpected to die within a bounded time. Observed in the I-gen-003 V4 Pro smoke: a single section-outline call produced `content=1791 bytes` + **`reasoning=17521 bytes`** (4790 reasoning tokens vs 5388 output tokens). The reasoning trace is roughly 3× the size of the final content.\n\n**Right now POLARIS discards the reasoning trace entirely.** `multi_section_generator._call_section` (and the outline/repair call sites) return `(response.content, input_tokens, output_tokens)` — `response.reasoning` is dropped on the floor. `LLMResponse` carries a `.reasoning` field but nothing in the generator or audit_ir persists it. A reviewer auditing a POLARIS report cannot see how the model reasoned its way to the cited prose.\n\nFor an audit-grade clinical tool this is a transparency hole: the reasoning IS evidence of process. It must be captured, stored SEPARATELY from the output content (never mixed into report.md / verified_text), and surfaced in the audit bundle.\n\n## Acceptance criteria\n\n1. **Capture** — `_call_section`, the outline call, and the I-bug-108 sentence-repair call each return / thread through `response.reasoning` alongside `content`. `SectionResult` (and the outline result) gain a `reasoning_trace` field.\n2. **Store separately** — per run, write a `reasoning_trace.jsonl` (or per-section files) into the run output dir, ONE record per LLM call: `{section, call_type (outline|section|repair|regen), attempt_n, model, reasoning_text, content_text, input_tokens, output_tokens, reasoning_tokens, timestamp}`. The reasoning text is NEVER merged into `report.md`, `verified_text`, or the verified-sentence stream — those stay content-only.\n3. **Manifest + bundle** — `manifest.json` references the reasoning trace file(s); the audit bundle (`bundle.tar.gz`) includes them so an external reviewer gets the reasoning alongside the report + provenance.\n4. **Transparency surface** — `/transparency` (or the bundle README) documents that the reasoning trace is captured + where it lives + that it is model-process evidence, not verified claims. (Frontend Inspector rendering of the trace MAY be a follow-up — the core requirement here is capture + separate storage + bundle inclusion.)\n5. **Separation invariant** — a test asserts the reasoning text never appears in `report.md` / verified prose, and that `strict_verify` is never run against reasoning text. Reasoning is process-transparency, NOT a claim source.\n6. **Works for all models** — for non-reasoning-first models `response.reasoning` is usually empty/None; the trace record still gets written (empty reasoning_text) so the artifact schema is uniform. No crash, no-op when reasoning absent.\n7. Codex diff review APPROVE.\n\n## Depends on\n\n- I-gen-003 (GH#495) — V4 Pro must actually run as the generator first. This Issue lands immediately after.\n\n## Out of scope\n\n- Frontend Inspector UI for browsing the reasoning trace — separate follow-up (the bundle inclusion + separate storage is the P0 transparency requirement; the UI is P1 polish).\n- Retroactively capturing reasoning for past runs.\n\n## Severity\n\nP0 for the Carney demo transparency story — POLARIS is positioned on auditable per-sentence faithfulness; \"we throw away the model's reasoning\" is a hole a sophisticated reviewer (or Carney's office) would find immediately.\n\n🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)","closedAt":"2026-05-17T10:49:08Z","createdAt":"2026-05-14T16:17:01Z","labels":[],"number":496,"state":"CLOSED","title":"I-gen-004 — capture + store V4 Pro reasoning trace separately from output content, transparently"},{"body":"## Context\n\nOperator directive 2026-05-14: POLARIS must use **DeepSeek V4 Pro** as the generator — it is the model named in the OVH H200 procurement spec, `docs/transparency.md`, `docs/carney_demo_runbook.md`, and the whole sovereignty narrative. Right now the working generator is `deepseek-v3.2-exp` because of I-bug-091.\n\n## Why V4 Pro is currently parked (I-bug-091, 2026-05-09)\n\n`src/polaris_graph/llm/openrouter_client.py:255-271` reverted `PG_GENERATOR_MODEL` from V4 Pro to V3.2-Exp. With no upper bound, pip's resolver pulls latest-1.x of each package, hits the langchain 1.x ecosystem's internal version coupling, and backtracks through the entire release history trying to find a compatible set — effectively a hang.\n\nThis did not surface when I-carney-005 shipped Dockerfile.v6 because langchain 1.x had not been released / the resolver still converged then. .codex\reprioritization_2026_05_20\review_iter1.txt:15294:.codex\I-cd-008\codex_brief_review_iter1.txt:2850:outputs\audit_2026_05_18\all_issues.json:1:[{"body":"## Problem\n\nClaude repeatedly ends a turn with a prose status/next-step report instead of\nthe next tool call, after finishing a unit of work (a PR merge, a grounded\nscan, \"the next step is X\"). The user has flagged this 5+ times\n(2026-05-07 → 2026-05-18: \"why you pause here? any valid reason? figure out a\nway to resolve this constant pausing behaviour\").\n\nEvery prior fix was a *rule* (CLAUDE.md §8.1 banned STATUS blocks; §8.2 banned\nprose-between-merge-and-branch; §8.3.10 banned named rationalizations) — and\neach failed because the pause is a decision made at stop-time and the agent\nrationalizes a fresh exception. Per `failure_28_commits`: \"promises don't\nwork; structural removal does.\" A rule the agent must remember and apply is\nnot structural. The only structural mechanism is one **external to the agent's\nin-the-moment judgment** — a Stop hook.\n\n## Proposed mechanism\n\nA Claude Code `Stop` hook (`.claude/hooks/`, project-scoped per\n`stop_hook_must_be_project_scoped`) that, when the agent attempts to end a\nturn, decides allow-vs-block:\n\n- **Allow the stop** if any of: the last message is a user turn awaiting a\n  reply; a `state/halt_*` marker exists; the transcript shows a Codex iter-5\n  REQUEST_CHANGES; `state/active_issue.json` shows no in-progress work.\n- **Block the stop** (return the `block` decision + a reason) if there is\n  in-progress issue-queue work and none of the allow conditions hold — inject\n  \"continue: the next output is a tool call advancing the issue, not prose.\"\n\n## Hard constraints (this area is delicate)\n\n- **Loop-safety is mandatory.** A Stop hook that always blocks creates an\n  infinite loop. It must bound the number of consecutive blocks and/or only\n  block when the transcript shows a *just-completed* sub-step. See\n  `feedback_no_unbounded_polling_loops`.\n- Per #524 (I-pin-001): the prior `stop_hook_v3.py` canonical-pin check was\n  **deliberately neutralized** for false-firing on Windows CRLF. This hook\n  must not repeat that — no false blocks; when uncertain, allow.\n- Must distinguish \"user just asked a question\" (allow) from \"agent finished a\n  PR and the queue has more\" (block) — the core discriminator.\n- Codex APPROVE on brief + diff; offline test of both the allow and block\n  paths before merge.\n\n## Acceptance\n\n- A `Stop` hook is wired in `.claude/settings.json`, project-scoped, that\n  blocks a turn-end during incomplete issue-queue work with no fired halt.\n- Loop-safety: bounded consecutive blocks; a documented test of the\n  infinite-loop guard.\n- The allow path (user question / halt marker / no active issue) is tested.\n- Does not re-introduce the CRLF false-fire class of bug.\n\nRefs: CLAUDE.md §8.1/§8.2/§8.3.10; `feedback_dont_pause_keep_executing`;\n`failure_28_commits`; #524 (prior stop-hook neutralization).","closedAt":null,"createdAt":"2026-05-18T07:28:59Z","labels":[],"number":589,"state":"OPEN","title":"I-ci-002 — Stop hook to block fake-pause turn-ends during incomplete issue-queue work"},{"body":"## Bug\n\nSurfaced by the #400 (I-beat-001) BEAT-BOTH §-1.1 line-by-line audit — `outputs/audits/I-beat-001/cross_review_v12.md`, claim Q3-C1.\n\nIn the POLARIS Q3 (workforce / gen-AI white-collar employment) report, the gen-AI occupational-exposure decimals **75.5% / 68.4% / 62.6%** are cited to **Goldman Sachs 2023**. The decimals do not match the Goldman Sachs 2023 baseline — they match the **PWBM 2025** (Penn Wharton Budget Model, Eloundou/BLS-derived) exposure table.\n\nDecimals are correct (PWBM published them); the **source attribution is wrong** (citation points at Goldman Sachs 2023).\n\n## Classification\n\nThis is **NOT a fabrication** — the numbers are real and traceable. Two issue-ID classes in the current queue do\nnot survive this:\n\n### 1. `-followup` issues collapse onto their parent\n\nIssues `I-rdy-019-followup` (#558), `I-rdy-010-followup` (#537),\n`I-rdy-011-followup` (#539), `I-rdy-016-followup` (#547),\n`I-rdy-017-followup` (#549), `I-gen-004-followup` (#561) — a branch\n`bot/I-rdy-019-followup-*` canonicalizes to `ISSUE_ID = I-rdy-019`, i.e. the\n**parent issue's** (#515's) already-merged `.codex/I-rdy-019/` directory.\nThe gate then reads the parent's `codex_diff.patch` and excludes the wrong\ndir from the canonical-diff computation.\n\n**Observed on #558 / PR #569:** `codex-required` failed —\n`declared (trailer): 17dd8d74…` (read from `.codex/I-rdy-019/codex_diff.patch`,\n#515's file) vs `actual: 63ccfc8f…` (canonical diff that did NOT exclude\n`.codex/I-rdy-019-followup/**`). A\ndetached fetch-backend task whose Playwright cleanup (`_try_crawl4ai` →\n`_safe_close_crawler` → `__aexit__`) is genuinely wedged would therefore\ndelay — in the theoretical worst case, indefinitely — `asyncio.run`\n**teardown**, *after* all artifacts and the manifest are already written.\n\nIn practice Playwright browser cleanup is slow-worst-case, not\ninfinite-worst-case (CDP has internal timeouts; the browser subprocess is\nSIGTERM'd by Playwright's own process management), so the detached task is\nexpected to die within a bounded time. Observed in the I-gen-003 V4 Pro smoke: a single section-outline call produced `content=1791 bytes` + **`reasoning=17521 bytes`** (4790 reasoning tokens vs 5388 output tokens). The reasoning trace is roughly 3× the size of the final content.\n\n**Right now POLARIS discards the reasoning trace entirely.** `multi_section_generator._call_section` (and the outline/repair call sites) return `(response.content, input_tokens, output_tokens)` — `response.reasoning` is dropped on the floor. `LLMResponse` carries a `.reasoning` field but nothing in the generator or audit_ir persists it. A reviewer auditing a POLARIS report cannot see how the model reasoned its way to the cited prose.\n\nFor an audit-grade clinical tool this is a transparency hole: the reasoning IS evidence of process. It must be captured, stored SEPARATELY from the output content (never mixed into report.md / verified_text), and surfaced in the audit bundle.\n\n## Acceptance criteria\n\n1. **Capture** — `_call_section`, the outline call, and the I-bug-108 sentence-repair call each return / thread through `response.reasoning` alongside `content`. `SectionResult` (and the outline result) gain a `reasoning_trace` field.\n2. **Store separately** — per run, write a `reasoning_trace.jsonl` (or per-section files) into the run output dir, ONE record per LLM call: `{section, call_type (outline|section|repair|regen), attempt_n, model, reasoning_text, content_text, input_tokens, output_tokens, reasoning_tokens, timestamp}`. The reasoning text is NEVER merged into `report.md`, `verified_text`, or the verified-sentence stream — those stay content-only.\n3. **Manifest + bundle** — `manifest.json` references the reasoning trace file(s); the audit bundle (`bundle.tar.gz`) includes them so an external reviewer gets the reasoning alongside the report + provenance.\n4. **Transparency surface** — `/transparency` (or the bundle README) documents that the reasoning trace is captured + where it lives + that it is model-process evidence, not verified claims. (Frontend Inspector rendering of the trace MAY be a follow-up — the core requirement here is capture + separate storage + bundle inclusion.)\n5. **Separation invariant** — a test asserts the reasoning text never appears in `report.md` / verified prose, and that `strict_verify` is never run against reasoning text. Reasoning is process-transparency, NOT a claim source.\n6. **Works for all models** — for non-reasoning-first models `response.reasoning` is usually empty/None; the trace record still gets written (empty reasoning_text) so the artifact schema is uniform. No crash, no-op when reasoning absent.\n7. Codex diff review APPROVE.\n\n## Depends on\n\n- I-gen-003 (GH#495) — V4 Pro must actually run as the generator first. This Issue lands immediately after.\n\n## Out of scope\n\n- Frontend Inspector UI for browsing the reasoning trace — separate follow-up (the bundle inclusion + separate storage is the P0 transparency requirement; the UI is P1 polish).\n- Retroactively capturing reasoning for past runs.\n\n## Severity\n\nP0 for the Carney demo transparency story — POLARIS is positioned on auditable per-sentence faithfulness; \"we throw away the model's reasoning\" is a hole a sophisticated reviewer (or Carney's office) would find immediately.\n\n🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)","closedAt":"2026-05-17T10:49:08Z","createdAt":"2026-05-14T16:17:01Z","labels":[],"number":496,"state":"CLOSED","title":"I-gen-004 — capture + store V4 Pro reasoning trace separately from output content, transparently"},{"body":"## Context\n\nOperator directive 2026-05-14: POLARIS must use **DeepSeek V4 Pro** as the generator — it is the model named in the OVH H200 procurement spec, `docs/transparency.md`, `docs/carney_demo_runbook.md`, and the whole sovereignty narrative. Right now the working generator is `deepseek-v3.2-exp` because of I-bug-091.\n\n## Why V4 Pro is currently parked (I-bug-091, 2026-05-09)\n\n`src/polaris_graph/llm/openrouter_client.py:255-271` reverted `PG_GENERATOR_MODEL` from V4 Pro to V3.2-Exp. With no upper bound, pip's resolver pulls latest-1.x of each package, hits the langchain 1.x ecosystem's internal version coupling, and backtracks through the entire release history trying to find a compatible set — effectively a hang.\n\nThis did not surface when I-carney-005 shipped Dockerfile.v6 because langchain 1.x had not been released / the resolver still converged then. .codex\I-ux-002\codex_brief_verdict_iter4.txt:6670:.codex\reprioritization_2026_05_20\review_iter1.txt:15205:.codex\I-cd-009\codex_brief_review_iter5.txt:6213:.codex\I-cd-008\codex_brief_review_iter1.txt:2850:outputs\audit_2026_05_18\all_issues.json:1:[{"body":"## Problem\n\nClaude repeatedly ends a turn with a prose status/next-step report instead of\nthe next tool call, after finishing a unit of work (a PR merge, a grounded\nscan, \"the next step is X\"). The user has flagged this 5+ times\n(2026-05-07 → 2026-05-18: \"why you pause here? any valid reason? figure out a\nway to resolve this constant pausing behaviour\").\n\nEvery prior fix was a *rule* (CLAUDE.md §8.1 banned STATUS blocks; §8.2 banned\nprose-between-merge-and-branch; §8.3.10 banned named rationalizations) — and\neach failed because the pause is a decision made at stop-time and the agent\nrationalizes a fresh exception. Per `failure_28_commits`: \"promises don't\nwork; structural removal does.\" A rule the agent must remember and apply is\nnot structural. The only structural mechanism is one **external to the agent's\nin-the-moment judgment** — a Stop hook.\n\n## Proposed mechanism\n\nA Claude Code `Stop` hook (`.claude/hooks/`, project-scoped per\n`stop_hook_must_be_project_scoped`) that, when the agent attempts to end a\nturn, decides allow-vs-block:\n\n- **Allow the stop** if any of: the last message is a user turn awaiting a\n  reply; a `state/halt_*` marker exists; the transcript shows a Codex iter-5\n  REQUEST_CHANGES; `state/active_issue.json` shows no in-progress work.\n- **Block the stop** (return the `block` decision + a reason) if there is\n  in-progress issue-queue work and none of the allow conditions hold — inject\n  \"continue: the next output is a tool call advancing the issue, not prose.\"\n\n## Hard constraints (this area is delicate)\n\n- **Loop-safety is mandatory.** A Stop hook that always blocks creates an\n  infinite loop. It must bound the number of consecutive blocks and/or only\n  block when the transcript shows a *just-completed* sub-step. The `policy` corpus\nis larger (many `federalregister.gov` / `regulations.gov` 25 000-char PDFs).\n\n## Hypothesis\n\nThe post-retrieval corpus-assembly stage (chunking / embedding / tier\nclassification of the fetched documents — `sentence-transformers` territory)\nhas **no per-document or overall wall-clock timeout**. A pathological\ndocument, or simply a large corpus, can wedge it indefinitely. (§8.4 also\nflags `sentence-transformers` as a heavy-ML path that should not run\nunbounded in an autonomous loop.)\n\n## Impact\n\n**Demo-fatal**, same severity class as #551 — a single query whose corpus is\nlarge or contains a pathological document hangs the whole run before it ever\nreaches generation. The #514 rehearsal cannot pass start-to-finish until\nthis is bounded.\n\n## Acceptance\n\n- The post-retrieval corpus-assembly stage honors a hard wall-clock bound\n  (per-document and/or overall); a wedged document is skipped or the stage\n  fails loud, and the run reaches a terminal verdict.\n- A regression test exercises a corpus-assembly step that hangs and asserts\n  the run is bounded.\n- Codex APPROVE.\n\n## Evidence\n\n#514 rehearsal run `rehearsal_policy_117dd77e` (run DB: `in_progress`,\n`pipeline_status=None`). Log `.codex/I-rdy-018/_live_run2.log` line ~1051\nshows `parallel_fetch` completing; no `[corpus]` line ever follows. Related:\n#551 (retrieval fan-out timeout), #552 (asyncio.run teardown), G9/G10.\n","closedAt":"2026-05-17T06:28:30Z","createdAt":"2026-05-17T04:37:46Z","labels":[],"number":554,"state":"CLOSED","title":"I-bug — pipeline-A: run hangs in post-retrieval corpus assembly (no per-document timeout)"},{"body":"Carved from #551 (I-bug-114) during its Codex review — the narrower residual\nthat #551's in-flight fix deliberately does not close.\n\n## Context\n\n#551 bounds the **in-flight** concurrent fetch fan-out in\n`AccessBypass.fetch_with_bypass`: a hung backend can no longer freeze\nretrieval mid-run (that was the demo-fatal symptom — the #514 rehearsal's\n`policy` run froze ~30 min in retrieval). #551 does this with a hard\n`asyncio.wait`-based wall-clock + a bounded cancel-grace window; a backend\nwhose cancellation cleanup exceeds the grace window is **detached**.\n\n## The residual gap\n\n`run_one_query` runs the whole pipeline under a single `asyncio.run(...)`.\nWhen it completes, `asyncio.run` calls `_cancel_all_tasks`, which cancels\nevery still-pending task and **awaits** them before closing the loop. A\ndetached fetch-backend task whose Playwright cleanup (`_try_crawl4ai` →\n`_safe_close_crawler` → `__aexit__`) is genuinely wedged would therefore\ndelay — in the theoretical worst case, indefinitely — `asyncio.run`\n**teardown**, *after* all artifacts and the manifest are already written.\n\nIn practice Playwright browser cleanup is slow-worst-case, not\ninfinite-worst-case (CDP has internal timeouts; the browser subprocess is\nSIGTERM'd by Playwright's own process management), so the detached task is\nexpected to die within a bounded time. But the correctness guarantee is not\nairtight.\n\n## Acceptance\n\n- A genuinely un-cancellable fetch backend cannot delay `asyncio.run`\n  teardown — e.g. run each browser-using backend in a daemon thread with\n  its own event loop (a daemon thread is not an asyncio task on the\n  retrieval loop, so teardown does not await it), or subprocess-isolate it.\n- A regression test models a backend that ignores cancellation entirely and\n  asserts the enclosing `asyncio.run` still tears down within a bound.\n- Codex APPROVE.\n\nDepends on: #551 merged. Related robustness scope: G9, G10. Lower priority\nthan #551 — the demo-fatal symptom (mid-run retrieval freeze) is #551's;\nthis is a post-artifacts teardown-correctness edge.\n","closedAt":null,"createdAt":"2026-05-17T01:27:32Z","labels":[],"number":552,"state":"OPEN","title":"I-bug — retrieval concurrency: asyncio.run teardown can await a detached fetch backend whose cancellation cleanup wedges"},{"body":"Surfaced by the #514 Phase-4 OpenRouter rehearsal live run (2026-05-17).\n\n## Symptom\n\nA pipeline-A run hangs **indefinitely** in retrieval. #!/usr/bin/env python3
"""Extract the slim verdict block from a raw ``codex exec`` transcript.

I-sec-001 (#535): per-issue Codex review artifacts must be committed as the
bounded §8.3.9 verdict block ONLY — never the full multi-MB transcript, which
routinely captures ``cat .env`` output and leaks credentials into git.

Subcommands:

  extract   Parse the final §8.3.9 verdict block out of a raw transcript,
            re-serialize it canonically (parse-then-emit, so NO trailing
            transcript text can survive), scan the result for secrets
            (shared pattern scanner + local ``.env`` value match), and write
            the slim artifact. Non-zero exit on any secret hit — the ship
            procedure then redacts before commit.

  validate  Confirm a committed file IS a schema-bounded slim verdict block
            (all 7 §8.3.9 keys, valid enums, byte-capped, no trailing
            content). Used by the CI .codex artifact gate; has NO ``.env``
            dependency so it runs identically on any runner.

Usage:
    python scripts/extract_codex_verdict.py extract <raw_transcript> --out <slim.txt>
    python scripts/extract_codex_verdict.py validate <slim.txt>
"""
from __future__ import annotations

import argparse
import re
import subprocess
import sys
import tempfile
from pathlib import Path

_REPO_ROOT = Path(__file__).resolve().parents[1]

# §8.3.9 verdict schema.
SCALAR_KEYS = ("verdict", "convergence_call")
LIST_KEYS = ("novel_p0", "continuing_p0", "p1", "p2",
             "remaining_blockers_for_execution")
ALL_KEYS = ("verdict", "novel_p0", "continuing_p0", "p1", "p2",
            "convergence_call", "remaining_blockers_for_execution")
VALID_VERDICT = {"APPROVE", "REQUEST_CHANGES"}
VALID_CONVERGENCE = {"continue", "accept_remaining"}

# A slim verdict artifact must never approach transcript size. A real verdict
# block is well under 2 KB; 8 KB leaves headroom for verbose findings while
# still being orders of magnitude below a raw transcript.
SLIM_BYTE_CAP = 8192

_KEY_RE = re.compile(
    r"^(?P<key>verdict|novel_p0|continuing_p0|p1|p2|"
    r"convergence_call|remaining_blockers_for_execution)\s*:\s*(?P<val>.*)$"
)
_ITEM_RE = re.compile(r"^\s*-\s?(?P<item>.*)$")


def _parse_inline_list(body: str) -> list[str]:
    """Parse the body of a YAML/JSON flow list (the text inside ``[...]``).

    Quote- and depth-aware comma split, so a non-empty inline list value such
    as ``p1: ["P1: a, b", "P1: c"]`` is parsed rather than silently dropped.
    """
    body = body.strip()
    if not body:
        return []
    items: list[str] = []
    buf: list[str] = []
    depth = 0
    quote: str | None = None
    for ch in body:
        if quote:
            if ch == quote:
                quote = None
            else:
                buf.append(ch)
            continue
        if ch in ('"', "'"):
            quote = ch
            continue
        if ch in "[{(":
            depth += 1
            buf.append(ch)
            continue
        if ch in "]})":
            depth -= 1
            buf.append(ch)
            continue
        if ch == "," and depth == 0:
            items.append("".join(buf).strip())
            buf = []
            continue
        buf.append(ch)
    if buf:
        items.append("".join(buf).strip())
    return [it for it in items if it]


def parse_verdict_block(text: str) -> dict | None:
    """Find the LAST §8.3.9 verdict block in ``text`` and parse it to a dict.

    Returns None if no well-formed block is found. Parsing (not regex
    copy-through) is what guarantees the re-serialized output carries no
    trailing transcript text (I-sec-001 brief §3.1).
    """
    lines = text.splitlines()
    starts = []
    for i, ln in enumerate(lines):
        m = _KEY_RE.match(ln.strip())
        if m and m.group("key") == "verdict" and m.group("val").strip() in VALID_VERDICT:
            starts.append(i)
    if not starts:
        return None

    parsed: dict = {}
    current_list: str | None = None
    for ln in lines[starts[-1]:]:
        stripped = ln.strip()
        if not stripped:
            continue  # blank lines tolerated between keys
        if stripped.startswith("```"):
            break  # closing code fence ends the block
        km = _KEY_RE.match(stripped)
        if km:
            key, val = km.group("key"), km.group("val").strip()
            if key in parsed:
                break  # a repeated key → the block already ended
            if key in SCALAR_KEYS:
                parsed[key] = val
                current_list = None
            else:
                # list key — three valid §8.3.9 forms:
                #   `key:`        block items follow on `  - ` lines
                #   `key: []`     empty list, nothing follows
                #   `key: [a, b]` non-empty inline flow list
                if val == "":
                    parsed[key] = []
                    current_list = key
                elif val == "[]":
                    parsed[key] = []
                    current_list = None
                elif val.startswith("[") and val.endswith("]"):
                    parsed[key] = _parse_inline_list(val[1:-1])
                    current_list = None
                else:
                    # malformed list-key value — reject loudly rather than
                    # silently drop content (codex diff review iter-1 P1).
                    return None
            continue
        im = _ITEM_RE.match(ln)
        if im is not None and current_list is not None:
            parsed[current_list].append(im.group("item").strip())
            continue
        if current_list and parsed.get(current_list) and ln.startswith((" ", "\t")):
            parsed[current_list][-1] = (
                parsed[current_list][-1] + " " + stripped).strip()
            continue
        break  # transcript prose → the block has ended

    if not all(k in parsed for k in ALL_KEYS):
        return None
    if parsed["verdict"] not in VALID_VERDICT:
        return None
    if parsed["convergence_call"] not in VALID_CONVERGENCE:
        return None
    return parsed


def serialize_verdict(d: dict) -> str:
    """Canonical re-serialization of a parsed verdict block (LF-terminated)."""
    out: list[str] = [f"verdict: {d['verdict']}"]
    for k in ("novel_p0", "continuing_p0", "p1", "p2"):
        items = d.get(k) or []
        if not items:
            out.append(f"{k}: []")
        else:
            out.append(f"{k}:")
            out.extend(f"  - {it}" for it in items)
    out.append(f"convergence_call: {d['convergence_call']}")
    rb = d.get("remaining_blockers_for_execution") or []
    if not rb:
        out.append("remaining_blockers_for_execution: []")
    else:
        out.append("remaining_blockers_for_execution:")
        out.extend(f"  - {it}" for it in rb)
    return "\n".join(out) + "\n"


def _load_env_secret_values(env_path: Path) -> list[str]:
    """Credential-shaped values from a local .env (values only, for substring
    matching — names dropped). Returns [] if .env is absent (e.g. on CI)."""
    if not env_path.is_file():
        return []
    secret_name = re.compile(r"(API_KEY|SECRET|TOKEN|PASSWORD|_KEY)$", re.I)
    vals: list[str] = []
    for line in env_path.read_text(encoding="utf-8", errors="replace").splitlines():
        line = line.strip()
        if not line or line.startswith("#") or "=" not in line:
            continue
        k, _, v = line.partition("=")
        v = v.strip().strip('"').strip("'")
        if (secret_name.search(k.strip()) and len(v) >= 20
                and not v.startswith(("http", "${", "/")) and " " not in v):
            vals.append(v)
    return vals


def scan_for_leaks(text: str, env_values: list[str]) -> list[str]:
    """Return leak descriptions — NEVER the secret value itself."""
    hits: list[str] = []
    scanner = _REPO_ROOT / "scripts" / "autoloop" / "scan_for_secrets.py"
    if scanner.is_file():
        with tempfile.NamedTemporaryFile(
                "w", suffix=".txt", delete=False, encoding="utf-8") as tf:
            tf.write(text)
            tmp = tf.name
        try:
            r = subprocess.run(
                [sys.executable, str(scanner), tmp, "--strict"],
                capture_output=True, text=True)

 succeeded in 6271ms:
"""Tests for the I-sec-001 (#535) codex-transcript leak-prevention tooling:
scripts/extract_codex_verdict.py and scripts/ci/codex_artifact_gate.py.
"""
from __future__ import annotations

import importlib.util
import sys
from pathlib import Path

_REPO = Path(__file__).resolve().parents[1]


def _load(modname: str, relpath: str):
    spec = importlib.util.spec_from_file_location(modname, _REPO / relpath)
    assert spec and spec.loader
    mod = importlib.util.module_from_spec(spec)
    sys.modules[modname] = mod
    spec.loader.exec_module(mod)
    return mod


ecv = _load("extract_codex_verdict", "scripts/extract_codex_verdict.py")
gate = _load("codex_artifact_gate", "scripts/ci/codex_artifact_gate.py")


# A realistic raw transcript: preamble + the verdict block + trailing prose.
_GOOD_RAW = """\
codex
some preamble reasoning the model emitted while exploring the repo ...
exec
git diff --stat

verdict: APPROVE
novel_p0: []
continuing_p0: []
p1: []
p2:
  - "P2: a minor naming nit"
convergence_call: accept_remaining
remaining_blockers_for_execution: []
tokens used
12345
trailing transcript prose that must NOT survive extraction
more trailing junk
"""


# ── extract ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────

def test_extract_writes_slim_block_only(tmp_path):
    raw = tmp_path / "raw.txt"
    raw.write_text(_GOOD_RAW, encoding="utf-8")
    out = tmp_path / "slim.txt"
    assert ecv.cmd_extract(raw, out) == 0
    text = out.read_text(encoding="utf-8")
    assert text.startswith("verdict: APPROVE")
    assert "trailing transcript prose" not in text
    assert "tokens used" not in text
    assert "some preamble reasoning" not in text
    assert "P2: a minor naming nit" in text
    assert ecv.cmd_validate(out) == 0  # the slim output round-trips


def test_extract_takes_the_LAST_verdict_block(tmp_path):
    # codex often prints the block twice; the final one wins.
    doubled = _GOOD_RAW + "\n" + (
        "verdict: REQUEST_CHANGES\nnovel_p0: []\ncontinuing_p0: []\n"
        "p1: []\np2: []\nconvergence_call: continue\n"
        "remaining_blockers_for_execution: []\n")
    raw = tmp_path / "raw.txt"
    raw.write_text(doubled, encoding="utf-8")
    out = tmp_path / "slim.txt"
    assert ecv.cmd_extract(raw, out) == 0
    assert out.read_text(encoding="utf-8").startswith("verdict: REQUEST_CHANGES")


def test_extract_blocks_secret_in_verdict(tmp_path):
    fake = "sk-" + "A1b2C3d4" * 6  # synthetic OpenAI-shaped key, not real
    raw = tmp_path / "raw.txt"
    raw.write_text(
        "verdict: REQUEST_CHANGES\nnovel_p0: []\ncontinuing_p0: []\n"
        f'p1:\n  - "leaked: {fake}"\np2: []\n'
        "convergence_call: continue\nremaining_blockers_for_execution: []\n",
        encoding="utf-8")
    out = tmp_path / "slim.txt"
    assert ecv.cmd_extract(raw, out) == 4  # secret-detected exit code
    assert not out.exists()               # nothing written on a leak


# ── parse / serialize ─────────────────────────────────────────────

def test_parse_returns_none_when_no_block():
    assert ecv.parse_verdict_block("just transcript text, no verdict") is None


def test_parse_returns_none_on_missing_keys():
    assert ecv.parse_verdict_block(
        "verdict: APPROVE\nnovel_p0: []\n") is None  # 5 keys missing


def test_parse_inline_nonempty_list_not_dropped():
    """Codex diff-review iter-1 P1: a non-empty inline flow list must be
    parsed, not silently dropped."""
    block = (
        'verdict: REQUEST_CHANGES\nnovel_p0: []\ncontinuing_p0: []\n'
        'p1: ["P1: first finding", "P1: second finding"]\np2: []\n'
        'convergence_call: continue\nremaining_blockers_for_execution: []\n')
    parsed = ecv.parse_verdict_block(block)
    assert parsed is not None
    assert parsed["p1"] == ["P1: first finding", "P1: second finding"]
    slim = ecv.serialize_verdict(parsed)
    assert "P1: first finding" in slim and "P1: second finding" in slim


def test_parse_inline_list_with_comma_inside_quotes():
    block = (
        'verdict: APPROVE\nnovel_p0: []\ncontinuing_p0: []\n'
        'p1: []\np2: ["P2: a, b, c is one item"]\n'
        'convergence_call: accept_remaining\n'
        'remaining_blockers_for_execution: []\n')
    parsed = ecv.parse_verdict_block(block)
    assert parsed is not None
    assert parsed["p2"] == ["P2: a, b, c is one item"]


def test_serialize_roundtrip_is_idempotent():
    parsed = ecv.parse_verdict_block(_GOOD_RAW)
    assert parsed is not None
    once = ecv.serialize_verdict(parsed)
    assert ecv.serialize_verdict(ecv.parse_verdict_block(once)) == once


# ── validate ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────

def test_validate_rejects_oversized(tmp_path):
    big = tmp_path / "big.txt"
    big.write_text("verdict: APPROVE\n" + "x" * (ecv.SLIM_BYTE_CAP + 10),
                   encoding="utf-8")
    assert ecv.cmd_validate(big) == 1


def test_validate_rejects_trailing_transcript(tmp_path):
    f = tmp_path / "trail.txt"
    f.write_text(_GOOD_RAW, encoding="utf-8")  # raw — has trailing prose
    assert ecv.cmd_validate(f) == 1

.codex\I-perm-010\s11_audit_stderr.txt:919:    "direct_quote": "Prebiotics are non-digestible fibers that act as food for the beneficial bacteria in your gut, helping them grow and thrive. These fibers are fermented in your colon, producing short-chain fatty acids (SCFAs) that fuel colon cells, balance gut bacteria, and support digestion, immunity, and even mental health. Key Benefits of Prebiotics: - Gut Health: Nourish good bacteria, improve digestion, and reduce bloating. - Immune Support: Boosts gut immunity and reduces inflammation. - Mental Health: Supports brain function and mood through the gut-brain axis. Where to Find Prebiotics: - Foods: Chicory root, garlic, onions, leeks, and bananas. - Supplements: Inulin, fructooligosaccharides (FOS), or galactooligosaccharides (GOS). - Fortified Products: Bars, powders, and drinks with added prebiotic fiber. Adults need 2.5\u201310 grams of prebiotics daily. Start small to avoid side effects like gas or bloating, and gradually increase intake through foods or supplements. Prebiotics work best when paired with probiotics, creating \"synbiotics\" that enhance gut health even further. Ready to improve your gut health? Let\u2019s dive into the details. Prebiotic Functions in the Gut How Fermentation Works Fermentation plays a key role in transforming prebiotics into compounds that improve digestion. In the colon, this process produces short-chain fatty acids (SCFAs) like acetate, propionate, and butyrate. These SCFAs not only provide energy but also activate important hormone pathways [[6]](https://www.nc\n\n[...]\n\n857/). These changes offer several protective benefits: - Fermentation strengthens the mucus layer. - Butyrate, a key SCFA, fuels colon cells, aids cell differentiation, and has anti-inflammatory properties [[8]](https://www.frontiersin.org/articles/10.3389/fmicb.2018.00031/pdf). Prebiotic fermentation also influences metabolism. Research shows that consuming inulin can increase the glycine-to-taurine conjugation ratio by up to 100% in study models [[3]](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC5739857/).
